comment.body:
I really respect this part of the message. “You always need to be critical. Whatever I write should be discussed, reviewed, expanded, or corrected if I’ve made a mistake. That will happen sometimes, there’s always room for that.”

Anything that I’ve written about involving last Thursday’s analysis has been rooted in this spirit.

Same with any other “critical” feedback I’ve mentioned in the past.

I have always quoted her posts, read them over several times, and have been sure to state where her specific analysis has seemed conflicted, contradictory, or conflated between multiple timeframes.

Many members have messaged me privately showing support when my messages have received negative feedback. Many have liked my posts on these threads, or have said themselves on these threads that they believe my “criticism” is rooted in Alma’s quote above.

I haven’t just been bombastic with bashing Alma or making shallow character assassinations without any substance. I have also praised her for the many things she’s done well.

But I am a “critical thinker”. And critical thinking is imperative to having a healthy society and for course correction. Critical thinking is the number 1 utility of free speech.

Now, in the spirit of genuine criticism and goodwill, I would like to invite any of you that believes my conduct to match Alma’s disdain above to do so now so we may clear the air. Because that hasn’t been my intent at all. And I would hope that would be self evident in the fact that I have quoted what she has said very specifically and directly laid out the context with specific references as to prevent confusion and misunderstanding.

comment.body: Don't be too harsh on yourself. This stuff is pretty complex. But in order to get the best out of this subscription you need to develop an intuition and a knowledge base about these things. Study the greeks one by one. ChatGPT and the like can be very valuable as well (although some stuff needs to be double checked). I think a lot of the confusion also arises due to a different mindset. There is no need to trade everyday and there is no need to catch every turn. You will burn yourself and you capital up way too quick this way. A lot of the more experienced people know this (and frankly most of them, including myself) learned the hard way. People overestimate how much of success in trading has nothing to do with strategy but with emotional control, patience, discipline etc.

comment.body:
Stress is a shortener of lives. Never worth it. Avoid negativity at all costs, for it has no humility in its selfish bluster. Be kind, no you're not the only one in the room and be willing to learn from others outside your comfortable or familiar circle - you'll be surprised how big the world is.

Ahhhhh, Europe, Alma. The diversity, the neighbour's of all sorts, etc. Sure, squabbles happen, but look at the rich cultures and sciences and institutions you create and experience. The insular thinking the world is unfortunately displaying too much of now is self-destructive... that's why it's important to shake someone's else's hands you have never met next time you sit down for coffee or wine or a beer, etc.

Have a great weekend all - and read something new!

comment.body: You need to think of the market as a random process that follows certain statistical properties and can be described by the moments of its distribution. Mean, median, standard deviation, skewness etc give you the process characteristics on which you can calculate an expected value (or simulate future paths). So question for you: if I observe a random process that has a standard deviation x, with what chance will my next observation (e.g the next 1 min close-to-close return) lie in the interval [-x, x]. If you can answer that you have the answer to your question

comment.body:
Those are just the % of 1st Std, 2nd std, 3rd std of a normal distribution curve. So if you have certain level of volatility you can work out how far the market should move that day away from its start point and assign a probability to it. If we are at 2nd std deviation down well you are below 95.4% of the possible down expectation and 100% of the possible up expectations. So in effect you are below 97.6% of the possible moves that day. So possibly not a bad place to try a long all other things being equal. But normal distribution don’t describe the real world, the real world has lumpy tails. 

I think these lumpy tails are modelled now in option pricing but in the old days I think some people made a lot of money by placing regular far OTM bets that Black Scholes would price using normal distributions. These people understood NDist was not a perfect approximation of reality
comment.raw_body:
Those are just the % of 1st Std, 2nd std, 3rd std of a normal distribution curve. So if you have certain level of volatility you can work out how far the market should move that day away from its start point and assign a probability to it. If we are at 2nd std deviation down well you are below 95.4% of the possible down expectation and 100% of the possible up expectations. So in effect you are below 97.6% of the possible moves that day. So possibly not a bad place to try a long all other things being equal. But normal distribution don’t describe the real world, the real world has lumpy tails. 

I think these lumpy tails are modelled now in option pricing but in the old days I think some people made a lot of money by placing regular far OTM bets that Black Scholes would price using normal distributions. These people understood NDist was not a perfect approximation of reality

comment.body: So at least in theory would it be more accurate to derive distribution from option pricing to get the "real" distribution?
comment.raw_body: So at least in theory would it be more accurate to derive distribution from option pricing to get the "real" distribution?

comment.body: Great question. The approach is fairly similar, but the option pricing always also includes market factors that influence the distribution. When there is a lot of supply on a certain point or area of the vol surface that distorts the distribution. Also it would make it by definition impossible to benefit from long or short vol because your market model and your option price model are based on the exact same distribution. What you want to profit from (the edge Alma is referring to) is the mispricing of the real odds, e.g. the market prices a different outcome than the underlying statistical process would have you expect. So in practice what you do is you configure the statistical process (the Wiener process/Brownian motion) to have fatter tails, for example by including random jumps). If I recall correctly Alma covered this in one of her first articles

So in practice what you do is you configure the statistical process (the Wiener process/Brownian motion) to have fatter tails, for example by including random jumps). comment.body: In the case of a normal distribution, theoretically yes; notwithstanding a "black swan" event to overwhelm the reduction of risk close to the tails.  Of course, every day with Trump has potential to disrupt the shape of an otherwise normal distribution
